One extremely powerful illustration of the sleuth versus bungler approach to influence comes from a keynote speech I was giving in Sydney, Australia. The audience was large, but as I was describing the principle of reciprocity, I noticed a man in the front row begin to squirm. As the keynote progressed, the man's face blanched and his hands started to shake on the table in front of him. And I thought, the guy might be ill. So I stopped my presentation to ask if he was okay. And he said, yeah, yeah, I'm fine. But that he had just had an unsettling realization of how he had bungled away the principle of reciprocity that cost him dearly. He said he owned a software company there in Sydney. And a while ago, he had gotten a call from the IT director of a company in Melbourne that was his biggest and longest standing customer. She was having a problem with his software and asked him to send a technician to Melbourne to fix it. Normally, for this type of problem, he would only send one technician. But because this was his biggest customer, the owner not only sent two of his best technicians, but he also went along himself to ensure that the customer's issues were resolved straightaway. It turned out the problem was not major and was fixed in just the course of the morning. As the owner and his team were packing up to leave, the IT director had a big smile on her face and asked the man to come into her office. She said, I can't thank you enough. Not only did you put two of your top techs on the next plane, but you came yourself. We don't normally see this kind of service from our vendors. The owner said he felt uncomfortable with that statement of gratitude. So to be polite, he said, oh, it was no big deal. Don't think a thing about it. We'd have done it for any of our customers, which caused his customer's face to drop. So he quickly added, oh, no, no, really, we love to come to Melbourne. The nightlife, the theater, the restaurants, we come here any chance we get. Don't worry. At that, he saw the customer's face turn to stone, after which the owner's company never got another piece of business from her again. Let's think about how it could be that after scoring a big victory with his biggest customer and gaining great appreciation for it, he trashed their future relationship. The software company owner thought he now knew, saying, because of what I claimed, she thought that we hadn't come to Melbourne for her, but instead for us. And it wasn't even true. With my clumsy statement, I dismissed the true efforts and expense that we had put in and bungled away the feelings of reciprocation that came along with those efforts. But I didn't know what to say. I was just trying to be a nice guy. So I said, no worries. And I made things even worse. How many times have we all heard ourselves say something very similar? We do something truly beyond the ordinary for another person, and we diminish its importance by waving our hands and saying, no problem or no big deal would have done it for anyone. Don't think a thing about it. Now, approaching the software owner's situation like a sleuth instead of a bungler, what might you say to get a better outcome than what he produced? Here are a few options. One, you might say, I was glad to help as rapidly as possible. For a valued customer of ours, so you can always expect this level of service. With this reply, you first confirm the uncommon levels of assistance you provided. You don't diminish or dismiss it. Second, you also declare that as a valued customer, the client will receive similar levels of competence and conscientiousness in all future business interactions. Now note that I am recommending using the term valued customer rather than biggest customer. After all, you want favorable future expectations to extend to all customers, not just the biggest one. Now alternatively, you could say, I was happy to be able to do this. Is there another department in your company that could benefit from this level of service? Perhaps while I'm here, you could introduce us. This kind of reply is ethical because it is based in the reality of the benefits you can provide. What's more, it offers an opportunity for the customer to reciprocate when the customer's appreciation is at top of mind. Finally, another possible response could be, you're welcome. After all, it's what long-term partners do for one another. Here, you recognize your genuine long-term partnership. Plus, you establish that this long-term partnership is not a one-way street. So in the future, if you need something from your client, perhaps to have an invoice paid more quickly than usual, he or she knows that it's what long-term partners do for one another. Think about the last time you did a favor for a client or colleague. Knowing what you now know, what could you have said to leverage the reciprocation principle ethically and effectively there?
